Piceatannol protects against age-related hearing loss by inhibiting cellular 
pyroptosis and inflammation through regulated 
Caspase11-GSDMD pathway 
Xu Yang a, Yanlin Wu b, Menglian Zhang a, Lingyu Zhang a, Tianhao Zhao a, Weiwei Qian a, 
Mengmei Zhu a, Xinya Wang a, Qiannuo Zhang a, Jiaqiang Sun b,*, Liuyi Dong a,** 
a Department of Pharmacology, Key Laboratory of Anti-Inflammatory and Immunopharmacology of Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chinese Medicine Research 
and Development of State Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Anhui Medical University, Hefei, Anhui, China 
b Department of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery, The First Affiliated Hospital of USTC, Hefei 230001, China   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Piceatannol 
ARHL 
Caspase-11 
Pyroptosis 
Inflammation 
A B S T R A C T   
Age-related hearing loss (ARHL) is a common issue associated with aging. One of the typical causes of hearing 
loss is the damage to inner ear hair cells. In addition, oxidative stress and inflammation contribute to ARHL. To 
avoid excessive inflammatory responses, non-classical scorch death pathway by cell membrane lipopolysac­
charide (LPS) activates of caspase-11. Piceatannol (PCT) is also known for anti-tumor, antioxidant and anti- 
inflammatory effects; however, the protective effect of piceatannol (PCT) on ARHL is unclear. The aim of this 
study was to elucidate the mechanism underlying protective effect of PCT on ARHL-induced inner ear hair cell 
damage. In vivo experiments showed that PCT could protect mice from inflammatory aging-induced hearing loss 
as well as from inner hair cells (IHC) and spiral ganglion (SG) deficits. In addition, inflammatory vesicle inhibitor 
BAY11–7082 ameliorated ARHL, inhibited NLRP3 and reduced GSDMD expression. In in vitro experiments we 
used LPS and D-gal to simulate the aging inflammatory environment. The results showed that intracellular 
reactive oxygen species levels, expression of Caspase-11, NLRP3, and GSDMD were significantly increased, yet 
treatment with PCT or BAY11–7082 significantly improved HEI-OC-1 cell injury while reducing inflammation- 
associated protein expression as well as the occurrence of pyroptosis. In conclusion, these results suggest a 
protective role for PCT against ARHL, possibly through Caspase-11-GSDMD pathway. Our findings may provide a 
new target and theoretical basis for hearing loss treatment using PCT.   
1. Introduction 
Age-related hearing loss (ARHL) is the most common sensory deficit 
disorder, affecting more than 65% of people over the age of 60 [1,2]. 
ARHL is characterized by a progressive decrease in hearing sensitivity, 
impairments in speech discrimination and sound localization, and de­
fects in central auditory processing [3,4]. The most characteristic 
changes include the loss of sensory hair cells and spiral ganglion (SG) 
neurons [5]. Although the mechanisms underpinning ARHL have not 
been fully elucidated, studies have shown that oxidative stress and 
inflammation are important molecular response mechanisms in the 
early stages of noise injury and ARHL development, involving increased 
levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and chronic systemic elevation of 
various inflammatory markers [6–8]. Caloric restriction and over­
expression of the endogenous cystathionine inhibitor XIAP in mice have 
been shown to regulate ARHL [9]. In general, pharmacological in­
terventions are considered to be beneficial in the treatment of hearing 
loss, and the cochleo protective effects of medications are of increasing 
interest to researchers. 
Inflammation is an important factor that contributes to the ARHL 
process. When inflammation occurs during aging, it may contribute to 
ARHL in humans; furthermore, elevated inflammation levels may lead to 
changes that contribute to cochlear pathology [10]. Inflammatory ves­

pro-inflammatory factors such as IL-1β and IL-18 [12]. It has been 
demonstrated that inflammation triggered by the activation of inflam­
matory vesicles plays an important role in the pathology of ARHL [13]. 
Pyroptosis is a form of programmed lytic cell death triggered by in­
flammatory cystathionases [14], Caspase-11 and pore-forming protein 
GSDMD are key proteins involved in pyroptosis, and an increasing 
number of studies focuses on Caspase-11-mediated non-classical 
pyroptosis pathway. Intracellular lipopolysaccharide (LPS) directly ac­
tivates caspase-11, and the activated caspase-11 activates GSDMD by 
cleaving it into N-terminal (GSDMD-N) and C-terminal (GSDMD-C) 
fragments, with GSDMD-N inserted into lipid bilayers to produce 
approximately 20 nm pores, resulting in cell swelling and lysis [15,16]. 
Growing evidence suggests an important link between pyroptosis and 
inflammation, Caspase-11 (or its human homologs caspase-4 and −5) 
induces cell death in cells that are highly inflammatory and promote 
tissue damage [17,18]. It has also been shown that Caspase-11 can 
directly activate NLRP3 inflammatory vesicles, and NLRP3 can sense 
various stimuli that interfere with intracellular homeostasis [19], while 
caspase-1, which is affected by inflammatory vesicles, also promotes the 
activation of GSDMD [20]. However, whether cell pyroptosis and its 
pathological changes in the cochlea of ARHL mice could be improved by 
inhibiting inflammatory vesicles is unclear. 
Piceatannol (PCT) is a phenolic compound mainly found in grape 
skins, passion fruit and red wine, with various pharmacological activ­
ities, such as anti-tumor, antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, 
which is an active ingredient in many Chinese medicines such as 
Rhubarb and Chinese wolfsbane [21]. A study showed that PCT treat­
ment significantly inhibited the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines 
and nitric oxide (NO) in RAW264.7 cells during LPS stimulation [22]. 
Although the anti-inflammatory effect of PCT has been demonstrated in 
different models, its role in the inflammatory response of the inner ear is 
unclear. Antioxidant therapies have had some success in treating noise 
hearing loss and ARHL models; however, research on the potential role 
of inflammation in ARHL remains scarce. Therefore, this study aimed to 
investigate the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects as well as the 
underlying mechanisms of PCT during inflammation in ARHL loss using 
a 48-week-old C57 mouse model. 
2. Materials and methods 
2.1. Animal handling 
Specific pathogen-free (SPF) male 48-week-old C57BL/6 (weighing 
35 ± 3 g) were obtained from Shanghai Family Planning Research 
Institute (license: SCXK (HU) 2018–0006, Shanghai, China). Specific 
pathogen-free (SPF) male 8-week-old C57BL/6 (weighing 20 ± 2 g) 
were obtained from Shanghai Family Planning Research Institute (li­
cense: SCXK (HU) 2018–0008, Shanghai, China). To facilitate animals’ 
adaptation to experimental conditions, all mice were housed at 22–23 ◦C 
with 60–75% humidity and under a 12:12 light/dark cycle, with food 
and water freely available. All animal experiments were performed in 
accordance with the National Institutes of Health (NIH) guidelines for 
the care and use of laboratory animals, and were approved by the 
Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Anhui Medical 
University. 
Mice were randomly divided into five groups (n = 6) in the phar­
macodynamic part: control group, 48-week-old geriatric group, geriatric 
+ PCT (2.5 mg/kg) group, geriatric + PCT (5 mg/kg) group, and geri­
atric + PCT (10 mg/kg) group. PCT was dissolved in distilled water as 
the treatment drug. PCT was injected intraperitoneally once a day for 7 
days. While modeling, the control group was given the same volume of 
distilled water daily. To further validate the results of the pharmaco­
dynamic part, we added a mechanistic part and divided the mice into 
four groups (n = 6): control group, 48-week-old geriatric group, geri­
atric + PCT (10 mg/kg) group, and geriatric + BAY11–7082 (10 mg/kg) 
group. PCT or BAY11–7082 were dissolved in distilled water as the 
treatment drug and injected intraperitoneally for 7 days. On day 8, after 
blood collection in the orbit, the mice were cervically dislocated and 
executed. After rapidly removing the cochlear tissues, some of the 
cochlear tissues were stored in a refrigerator at −80 ◦C for Western blot 
analysis, and the remaining tissues were placed in 4% paraformaldehyde 
for subsequent histological examination. The whole experiment was 
approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Anhui Medical University 
(LLSC20160183) and performed in accordance with the Guide for the 
Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. 
2.2. Auditory brainstem response 
After the modeling completion, the mice in each group were tested 
for auditory brainstem response (ABR). The mice were anesthetized by 
intraperitoneal injection of 3% sodium pentobarbital solution (30 mg/ 
kg) and placed on a heating pad, with temperature maintained at 37 ◦C 
in a compartment. Subcutaneous needle electrodes were inserted under 
the vertex (active), left ear (reference) and right ear (ground). Broad­
band clicks (10 ms, 22/s) and tone bursts (8, 12, 16, 24, 32 kHz, 0.5 ms 
rise/fall cycle, no plateau period, alternating phase) were used as stimuli 
using the Tucker Davis Technology (TDT) System III hardware and 
Biosig software (Tucker-Davis Technologies, 26 Alachua, USA) to record 
the ABR thresholds. Mean Upton 521 responses were obtained for each 
stimulus level. Thresholds for each frequency were determined by 
decreasing the intensity in 10 dB increments until no tissue response was 
detected, and ABR wave I was used to identify the ABR thresholds for 
each frequency. 
2.3. Hematoxylin-Eosin staining 
Histopathological damage to the cochlea was assessed using 
hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining. Mouse cochlear tissues were fixed in 
4% paraformaldehyde for 24 h. Cochlear specimens were dehydrated, 
dewaxed, embedded, and then cut into sections of 4 µm thickness. 
Sections were treated with xylene I/II and gradient ethanol, then stained 
with hematoxylin for 2 min. The sections were rinsed with running 
water and then stained with eosin for 30 s. Finally, the sections were 
desiccated and sealed. The cochlear tissue sections were inspected and 
photographed using a light microscope (Nikon, Japan). 
2.4. Immunohistochemistry 
After dewaxing, the Paraffin sections (n = 4) were incubated in 3% 
H2O2 for 20 min at room temperature. The sections were then placed in 
boiling sodium citrate buffer for 8 min and allowed to cool naturally, 
and sections were then washed 3 times with PBS. The sections were 
blocked with goat serum at 37 ◦C for 30 min. The rabbit polyclonal anti- 
NLRP3 (Affinity, DF7438, 1:200) and rabbit polyclonal anti-Caspase-11 
(Affinity, DF7609, 1:200) were added to the sections and incubated 
overnight at 4 ℃. The next day, the sections were first incubated with 
polymer-conjugated peroxidase-labeled goat anti-rabbit IgG for 1 h at 
37 ◦C. Subsequently, drops of DAB solution were added for color 
development, stained with hematoxylin, and finally, the sections were 
sealed with neutral resin. The sections were observed under the Intel­
ligent Tissue Section Imaging Analysis System. The expression of NLRP3 
or Caspase-11 in the region (200 ×) in each cochlear tissue section was 
detected using Image-pro Plus 6.0 analysis software. 
2.5. Western blotting 
The mice were sacrificed after the last ABR recording. Mouse 
cochlear samples or HEI-OC1 cells were lysed with lysis buffer (RIPA) 
and phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (PMSF) on ice for 30 min. The 
cochlear tissue was grounded using an automatic sample grinder 
(Shanghai Jinxing) for 120 s, and the sample was centrifuged at 12,000

demonstrated that inflammation triggered by the activation of inflam­
matory vesicles plays an important role in the pathology of ARHL [13]. 
Pyroptosis is a form of programmed lytic cell death triggered by in­
flammatory cystathionases [14], Caspase-11 and pore-forming protein 
GSDMD are key proteins involved in pyroptosis, and an increasing 
number of studies focuses on Caspase-11-mediated non-classical 
pyroptosis pathway. Intracellular lipopolysaccharide (LPS) directly ac­
tivates caspase-11, and the activated caspase-11 activates GSDMD by 
cleaving it into N-terminal (GSDMD-N) and C-terminal (GSDMD-C) 
fragments, with GSDMD-N inserted into lipid bilayers to produce 
approximately 20 nm pores, resulting in cell swelling and lysis [15,16]. 
Growing evidence suggests an important link between pyroptosis and 
inflammation, Caspase-11 (or its human homologs caspase-4 and −5) 
induces cell death in cells that are highly inflammatory and promote 
tissue damage [17,18]. It has also been shown that Caspase-11 can 
directly activate NLRP3 inflammatory vesicles, and NLRP3 can sense 
various stimuli that interfere with intracellular homeostasis [19], while 
caspase-1, which is affected by inflammatory vesicles, also promotes the 
activation of GSDMD [20]. However, whether cell pyroptosis and its 
pathological changes in the cochlea of ARHL mice could be improved by 
inhibiting inflammatory vesicles is unclear. 
Piceatannol (PCT) is a phenolic compound mainly found in grape 
skins, passion fruit and red wine, with various pharmacological activ­
ities, such as anti-tumor, antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, 
which is an active ingredient in many Chinese medicines such as 
Rhubarb and Chinese wolfsbane [21]. A study showed that PCT treat­
ment significantly inhibited the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines 
and nitric oxide (NO) in RAW264.7 cells during LPS stimulation [22]. 
Although the anti-inflammatory effect of PCT has been demonstrated in 
different models, its role in the inflammatory response of the inner ear is 
unclear. Antioxidant therapies have had some success in treating noise 
hearing loss and ARHL models; however, research on the potential role 
of inflammation in ARHL remains scarce. Therefore, this study aimed to 
investigate the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects as well as the 
underlying mechanisms of PCT during inflammation in ARHL loss using 
a 48-week-old C57 mouse model.

were obtained from Shanghai Family Planning Research Institute (li­
cense: SCXK (HU) 2018–0008, Shanghai, China). To facilitate animals’ 
adaptation to experimental conditions, all mice were housed at 22–23 ◦C 
with 60–75% humidity and under a 12:12 light/dark cycle, with food 
and water freely available. All animal experiments were performed in 
accordance with the National Institutes of Health (NIH) guidelines for 
the care and use of laboratory animals, and were approved by the 
Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Anhui Medical 
University.

rpm for 10 min at 4 ◦C. The supernatant containing the total protein was 
collected. After adding the protein buffer, the sample was denatured at 
100 ◦C for 10 min and stored at −
20 ◦C. A BCA protein detection kit 
(Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, Beijing, China) was used to mea­
sure the protein concentration. Proteins were separated on a 12% 
SDS–PAGE system at 120 V for one hour. The separated proteins were 
transferred onto a PVDF membrane and blocked with 5% skimmed milk 
at 25 ◦C for one hour. The membranes were incubated with antibodies 
against NLRP3, ASC, NF-KB, IL-18, IL-1β, GSDMD, and cleaved caspase- 
4/11 (Affinity, China) at 4 ◦C overnight, washed three times with TBST, 
and then incubated with goat anti-rabbit antibodies (ZB-2306, China). 
GAPDH (TA-09, ZSGB-BIO, China) was used as the loading control. The 
protein bands were detected and imaged on the ChemiQ4600 mini 
chemiluminescence imaging system (Bioshine, China), and the relative 
band intensities were quantified using ImageJ. 
2.6. Cell culture 
HEI-OC-1 cell was used for in vitro experiments and obtained free of 
charge from the Otorhinolaryngology Laboratory of the First Affiliated 
Hospital of the University of Science and Technology of China. HEI-OC1 
cells were grown in DMEM/high glucose (HyClone, USA) media sup­
plemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Cell Max. Australia) and 
1% penicillin/streptomycin. These were incubated in an incubator 
(Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) at 37 ◦C with 5% CO2, and grown till 
80% confluency. The cells were passaged using 0.25% trypsin. LPS 
(L2880, Sigma) and D-gal (G0750, Sigma) were used to damage HEI- 
OC1 cells at 1 μg/mL and 30 mg/mL concentrations. Cells were 
cultured in DMEM/high glucose without FBS and placed in the incu­
bator. HEI-OC1 cells were randomly divided into four groups: control, 
LPS+D-gal, pretreatment with PCT before LPS+D-gal-treated (PCT+
LPS+D-gal), and pretreatment with BAY11–7082 before LPS+D-gal 
treatment (BAY11–7082 + LPS+D-gal). 
2.7. Cell viability assay 
HEI-OC1 cells were inoculated and cultured in 96-well plates. After 
drug treatment, 90 μL of high sugar medium without FBS with 10 μL of 
CCK-8 assay reagent (cell viability assay using CCK-8 cell counting kit 
(Dojindo Laboratories, Japan), Japan laboratory) was added per well 
and the cells were incubated for 48 h. At the end of incubation, absor­
bance values were measured at 450 nm based on a multifunctional 
enzyme marker. 
2.8. Detection of ROS 
The Reactive Oxygen Species Assay Kit (ROS Assay Kit) is a kit used 
for the detection of ROS using the fluorescent probe, DCFH-DA. HEI-OC1 
cells were inoculated into 24-well plates. After treatment, the culture 
medium was gently aspirated and the cells were washed once with PBS 
buffer. Then, 500 μL of DCFH-DA staining solution was added to the cells 
at a final concentration of 10 μM and incubated at 37 ◦C for 30 min. The 
staining solution was discarded and the cells were washed thrice with 
the serum-free medium. These HEI-OC1 cells were photographed and 
the ROS levels were determined by measuring the intensity under a 
fluorescence inverted microscope (ZEISS LSM880 with Airyscan, Carl 
Zeiss AG, Germany). 
2.9. Flow cytometry 
After the proper treatment, cells from each group were harvested and 
200 μL of binding buffer was added to a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube to obtain 
a cell suspension. Then, 10 μL DCFH-DA was added, and the cells were 
stained for 20 min. PBS washing was repeated three times by centrifu­
gation, and finally, cellular ROS levels were assessed using flow 
cytometry. 
2.10. Immunofluorescence staining 
Expression of caspase-4/11 and GSDMD was detected by immuno­
fluorescence. First, HEI-OC-1 cells were immobilized with 4% para­
formaldehyde for 30 min and washed completely with PBS. After 
permeabilization with Triton X-100 (0.25%) for 30 min and blocking 
with 1% BSA in PBS (Sigma-Aldrich; Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Ger­
many), the HEI-OC-1 cells were treated with caspase-4/11 and GSDMD 
primary antibodies (Affinity, DF7609, 1: 500; Affinity, AF4017, 1:500, 
China) at 4 ◦C overnight. Subsequently, HEI-OC-1 cells were incubated 
with FITC-conjugated secondary antibody (cat. no. ZF-0311; lot 127805; 
1:200; ZSGB-BIO; OriGene Technologies, Inc., Beijing, China) for 1 h 
protected from light. Intracellular protein expression changes can be 
observed in inverted fluorescence microscopy with different intensities 
of green fluorescence, and randomly selected spots were observed and 
photographed to represent the caspase-4/11 and GSDMD expression. 
2.11. Scanning electron microscope 
After the completion of cell modeling, the slides were rinsed one by 
one into a vial containing 0.1 mol/L PH7.2 phosphate buffer for 1 h, 
changed 3–4 times, fixed in 4 ◦C fixative (5% glutaraldehyde) as soon as 
possible, rinsed and soaked 3 times with 0.1 M phosphate buffer 
(PH=7.2) at room temperature for 20 min each time for sample dehy­
dration, followed by sequential washing with 30%, 50%, 70%, and 90% 
ethanol for 15 min each. Then the samples were washed with 30, 50, 70, 
80, 90% ethanol for 15 min each, and finally with the addition of 100% 
ethanol (2 times) for 20 min/time, after which the samples were sent. 
2.12. Statistical analyses 
The GraphPad Prism 6 software was used for all statistical analyses. 
One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and t-tests were used to analyze 
and compare all results. P-value < 0.05 was considered statistically 
significant. All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. 
3. Results 
3.1. PCT attenuates hearing loss and cochlear structural changes in ARHL 
mice 
ABR measurements were performed after the mice reached the age of 
48 weeks. The results showed that the ARHL mouse model was suc­
cessfully established (Fig. 1A-B). The ABR measurements showed that 
the hearing threshold was significantly higher in the 48-week-old group 
than in the control group, indicating a significant decrease in hearing 
and a more severe hearing loss at high frequencies than at low fre­
quencies, while treatment with PCT reduced the ABR thresholds at click 
4 kHz, 8 kHz, 16 kHz, 24 kHz and 32 kHz (Fig. 1C-G). Therefore, PCT has 
a protective effect on hearing in mice. We next performed histological 
analysis of cochlear cross-sections from juvenile and aged mice, and 
juvenile mice maintained a better overall cochlear cell structure than 
aged mice did, whereas aged mice had significant inflammatory cell 
infiltration, loss of inner hair cells, and severe SG nerve degeneration. 
These parameters were significantly improved in the PCT (2.5 mg/kg, 5 
mg/kg, 10 mg/kg) pretreatment group (Fig. 1H). 
3.2. PCT attenuates inflammation and pyroptosis in the cochlea of ARHL 
mice 
We determined the expression of Caspase-11, NLRP3, and GSDMD 
using immunohistochemistry (Fig. 2A-B, F-G and S1 A-B). These ex­
periments showed that the expression of Caspase-11 and NLRP3 was 
significantly higher in aged mice than young mice, while the staining 
intensity of SG neurons was lower in PCT-pretreated aged mice, sug­
gesting the presence of more potent antioxidant processes. In (Fig. 2C-E,

mice 
ABR measurements were performed after the mice reached the age of 
48 weeks. The results showed that the ARHL mouse model was suc­
cessfully established (Fig. 1A-B). The ABR measurements showed that 
the hearing threshold was significantly higher in the 48-week-old group 
than in the control group, indicating a significant decrease in hearing 
and a more severe hearing loss at high frequencies than at low fre­
quencies, while treatment with PCT reduced the ABR thresholds at click 
4 kHz, 8 kHz, 16 kHz, 24 kHz and 32 kHz (Fig. 1C-G). Therefore, PCT has

infiltration, loss of inner hair cells, and severe SG nerve degeneration.

than in the control group, indicating a significant decrease in hearing 
and a more severe hearing loss at high frequencies than at low fre­
quencies, while treatment with PCT reduced the ABR thresholds at click

The Reactive Oxygen Species Assay Kit (ROS Assay Kit) is a kit used 
for the detection of ROS using the fluorescent probe, DCFH-DA. HEI-OC1 
cells were inoculated into 24-well plates. After treatment, the culture 
medium was gently aspirated and the cells were washed once with PBS 
buffer. Then, 500 μL of DCFH-DA staining solution was added to the cells 
at a final concentration of 10 μM and incubated at 37 ◦C for 30 min. The 
staining solution was discarded and the cells were washed thrice with 
the serum-free medium. These HEI-OC1 cells were photographed and 
the ROS levels were determined by measuring the intensity under a 
fluorescence inverted microscope (ZEISS LSM880 with Airyscan, Carl 
Zeiss AG, Germany). 
2.9. Flow cytometry 
After the proper treatment, cells from each group were harvested and 
200 μL of binding buffer was added to a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube to obtain 
a cell suspension. Then, 10 μL DCFH-DA was added, and the cells were 
stained for 20 min. PBS washing was repeated three times by centrifu­
gation, and finally, cellular ROS levels were assessed using flow 
cytometry. 
2.10. Immunofluorescence staining 
Expression of caspase-4/11 and GSDMD was detected by immuno­
fluorescence. First, HEI-OC-1 cells were immobilized with 4% para­
formaldehyde for 30 min and washed completely with PBS. After 
permeabilization with Triton X-100 (0.25%) for 30 min and blocking 
with 1% BSA in PBS (Sigma-Aldrich; Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Ger­
many), the HEI-OC-1 cells were treated with caspase-4/11 and GSDMD 
primary antibodies (Affinity, DF7609, 1: 500; Affinity, AF4017, 1:500, 
China) at 4 ◦C overnight. Subsequently, HEI-OC-1 cells were incubated 
with FITC-conjugated secondary antibody (cat. no. ZF-0311; lot 127805; 
1:200; ZSGB-BIO; OriGene Technologies, Inc., Beijing, China) for 1 h 
protected from light. Intracellular protein expression changes can be 
observed in inverted fluorescence microscopy with different intensities 
of green fluorescence, and randomly selected spots were observed and 
photographed to represent the caspase-4/11 and GSDMD expression. 
2.11. Scanning electron microscope 
After the completion of cell modeling, the slides were rinsed one by 
one into a vial containing 0.1 mol/L PH7.2 phosphate buffer for 1 h, 
changed 3–4 times, fixed in 4 ◦C fixative (5% glutaraldehyde) as soon as 
possible, rinsed and soaked 3 times with 0.1 M phosphate buffer 
(PH=7.2) at room temperature for 20 min each time for sample dehy­
dration, followed by sequential washing with 30%, 50%, 70%, and 90% 
ethanol for 15 min each. Then the samples were washed with 30, 50, 70, 
80, 90% ethanol for 15 min each, and finally with the addition of 100% 
ethanol (2 times) for 20 min/time, after which the samples were sent. 
2.12. Statistical analyses 
The GraphPad Prism 6 software was used for all statistical analyses. 
One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and t-tests were used to analyze 
and compare all results. P-value < 0.05 was considered statistically 
significant. All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. 
3. Results 
3.1. PCT attenuates hearing loss and cochlear structural changes in ARHL 
mice 
ABR measurements were performed after the mice reached the age of 
48 weeks. The results showed that the ARHL mouse model was suc­
cessfully established (Fig. 1A-B). The ABR measurements showed that 
the hearing threshold was significantly higher in the 48-week-old group 
than in the control group, indicating a significant decrease in hearing 
and a more severe hearing loss at high frequencies than at low fre­
quencies, while treatment with PCT reduced the ABR thresholds at click 
4 kHz, 8 kHz, 16 kHz, 24 kHz and 32 kHz (Fig. 1C-G). Therefore, PCT has 
a protective effect on hearing in mice. We next performed histological 
analysis of cochlear cross-sections from juvenile and aged mice, and 
juvenile mice maintained a better overall cochlear cell structure than 
aged mice did, whereas aged mice had significant inflammatory cell 
infiltration, loss of inner hair cells, and severe SG nerve degeneration. 
These parameters were significantly improved in the PCT (2.5 mg/kg, 5 
mg/kg, 10 mg/kg) pretreatment group (Fig. 1H). 
3.2. PCT attenuates inflammation and pyroptosis in the cochlea of ARHL 
mice 
We determined the expression of Caspase-11, NLRP3, and GSDMD 
using immunohistochemistry (Fig. 2A-B, F-G and S1 A-B). These ex­
periments showed that the expression of Caspase-11 and NLRP3 was 
significantly higher in aged mice than young mice, while the staining 
intensity of SG neurons was lower in PCT-pretreated aged mice, sug­
gesting the presence of more potent antioxidant processes. In (Fig. 2C-E,

3.3. BAY11-7082 attenuates hearing loss and cochlear structural changes 
in ARHL mice 
We have previously demonstrated the protective effect of PCT on 
ARHL, and to further verify that PCT alleviates ARHL by reducing 
inflammation in the organism, we subsequently added the inflammatory 
vesicle inhibitor BAY11–7082. Hearing was examined in each group of 
mice using ABR assay (Fig. 3A-G). These experiments showed a signif­
icant increase in hearing thresholds in the 48-week-old aged group 
compared to the control group, indicating the hearing thresholds in mice 
in the PCT (10 mg/kg) or BAY11–7082 (5 mg/kg) pretreatment groups 
were significantly lower than those in the model group; thus, both PCT 
or BAY11–7082 had a protective effect on hearing of mice. In contrast, 
the young mice maintained a better overall cochlear cell structure than 
the old mice, while the old mice had a significant loss of inner hair cells 
and SG in the cochlear structure, which was significantly improved in 
the PCT or BAY11–7082 pretreatment groups (Fig. 3H). 
3.4. BAY11-7082 attenuates inflammation and pyroptosis in the cochlea 
of ARHL mice 
We determined the expression of Caspase-11, NLRP3, and GSDMD 
using immunohistochemistry (Fig. 4A-B, F-G and S1 C-D). The expres­
sion of Caspase-11, a major protein involved in pyroptosis, and NLRP3, 
an essential protein of inflammation, was significantly higher in aged 
mice than in young mice, while the corresponding expression levels 
were lower in SG neurons in aged mice pretreated with PCT or 
BAY11–7082, indicating that these animals had more robust antioxidant 
mechanisms. In (Fig. 4C-E, K, M and N), the expression of pyroptosis 
proteins such as Caspase-11, Caspase-1, GSDMD, IL-18 and IL-1β was 
significantly higher in the cochlear tissues of aged mice than in those of 
the younger group of mice. The corresponding protein levels were 
significantly decreased after the treatment with PCT or BAY11–7082 
compared with the older group of mice. As shown in Fig. 4H-J and L, the 
expression of the NLRP3, NF-KB, and ASC in the cochlear tissue was 
significantly increased; the expression of inflammatory proteins was 
significantly decreased after administration of PCT or BAY11–7082 
compared with the older group.

3.5. Construction of HE-OC-1 aging inflammation model induced by D- 
gal and lipopolysaccharide 
To construct a D-gal-induced aging model of HE-OC-1 cells, we 
determined the optimal D-gal regimen for inducing HEI-OC-1 damage by 
using a range of different D-gal concentrations (5, 15, 30, and 45 mg/ 
mL) for 48 h (Fig. 5A). CCK-8 assay determined 30 mg/mL as the 
optimal concentration, and we subsequently used in situ staining by 
β-galactosidase reagent to detect the expression of β-galactosidase. As 
shown in Fig. 5B the staining was significantly stronger in the D-gal 
30 mg/mL treated group than in the Control group, demonstrating that 
at D-gal 30 mg/mL was sufficient to induce cellular senescence, and 
thus, the HEI-OC-1 cell aging model was successfully established. Sub­
sequently, in order to construct a model of lipopolysaccharide (LPS)- 
induced inflammation in HEI-OC-1 cells, we used 1 μg/mL LPS for 48 h 
according to previously published literature suggesting that it should 
lead to an appropriate level of damage and inflammatory activation in 
OC-1 cells [23]. We used 1 μg/mL LPS in combination with 30 mg/mL 
D-gal for 48 h to complete the establishment of the aging inflammation 
model. 
3.6. Effect of PCT on the viability of HE-OC-1 cells 
HEI-OC-1 cells were co-cultured with different concentrations of PCT 
(0.625, 1.25, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160 and 320 μM) for 48 h. The 
results showed that increasing the PCT concentration to 160 μM did not 
cause significant cytotoxicity compared with the control group 
(Fig. 5 C). HEI-OC-1 cells treated with PCT (2.5, 5 and 10 μM) were 
incubated for 4 h and then treated with LPS in combination with D-gal 
(Fig. 5D). The results showed that cell viability was significantly reduced 
in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treated group compared with 
the control group. Furthermore, PCT pretreatment significantly reduced 
cell damage and increased HEI-OC-1 cell viability compared with the 
LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treatment. The above results sug­
gest that PCT can reduce cellular senescence inflammatory damage. 
3.7. PCT reduces pyroptosis in HE-OC-1 cell induced by D-gal and LPS 
The results of cell immunofluorescence staining showed that the 
intracellular Caspase-11 protein fluorescence intensity was significantly 
stronger in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) group than in the 
control group. Compared with the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) 
group, the combined PCT (2.5, 5, and 10 μM) treatment group resulted 
in a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity. The pyroptosis 
mediator protein GSDMD showed the same trend (Fig. 6C and D). 
Western blotting results (Fig. 6E-J) showed that the expression of the 
pyroptosis proteins Caspase-4/11, Caspase-1, GSDMD, and the pro- 
inflammatory factors IL-1β and IL-18 were significantly increased in 
the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) group compared with the con­

OC-1 cells [23]. We used 1 μg/mL LPS in combination with 30 mg/mL 
D-gal for 48 h to complete the establishment of the aging inflammation 
model. 
3.6. Effect of PCT on the viability of HE-OC-1 cells 
HEI-OC-1 cells were co-cultured with different concentrations of PCT 
(0.625, 1.25, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160 and 320 μM) for 48 h. The 
results showed that increasing the PCT concentration to 160 μM did not 
cause significant cytotoxicity compared with the control group 
(Fig. 5 C). HEI-OC-1 cells treated with PCT (2.5, 5 and 10 μM) were 
incubated for 4 h and then treated with LPS in combination with D-gal 
(Fig. 5D). The results showed that cell viability was significantly reduced 
in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treated group compared with 
the control group. Furthermore, PCT pretreatment significantly reduced 
cell damage and increased HEI-OC-1 cell viability compared with the 
LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treatment. The above results sug­
gest that PCT can reduce cellular senescence inflammatory damage. 
3.7. PCT reduces pyroptosis in HE-OC-1 cell induced by D-gal and LPS

intracellular Caspase-11 protein fluorescence intensity was significantly 
stronger in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) group than in the 
control group. Compared with the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) 
group, the combined PCT (2.5, 5, and 10 μM) treatment group resulted 
in a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity. The pyroptosis 
mediator protein GSDMD showed the same trend (Fig. 6C and D). 
Western blotting results (Fig. 6E-J) showed that the expression of the 
pyroptosis proteins Caspase-4/11, Caspase-1, GSDMD, and the pro- 
inflammatory factors IL-1β and IL-18 were significantly increased in 
the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) group compared with the con­
trol group. PCT (2.5, 5, and 10 μM) combination treatment groups all

3.8. PCT reduces inflammatory injury and ROS levels in HE-OC-1 cells 
induced by D-gal and LPS 
The results of cellular immunofluorescence staining showed (Fig. 7A 
and B) that NLRP3 expression was higher in HEI-OC-1 cells in the LPS 
(1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) group than in the control group. 
Meanwhile, PCT treatment inhibited the upregulation of intracellular 
NLRP3 expression and weakened the fluorescence intensity compared 
with the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treatment alone. Western 
blotting (Fig. 7C-F) showed that the expression of NLRP3, NF-KB, and 
ASC, was significantly increased in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/ 
mL) group and significantly decreased in the PCT (2.5, 5, and 10 μM) 
combination treatment group in a concentration-dependent manner 
compared to the control group. We subsequently measured ROS levels 
using DCFH-DA fluorometric assay, and the results showed that ROS 
levels were significantly increased in HEI-OC-1 cells in the LPS (1 μg/ 
mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treatment group compared to the control 
group, and the combined administration of PCT (2.5, 5, and 10 μM) 
significantly reduced ROS levels (Fig. 7I and J). In addition, to further 
verify the ROS levels, we measured these levels in HEI-OC-1 cells using 
flow cytometry, and the peak in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) 
treatment group was significantly shifted to the right compared to the 
control group, indicating an increase in intracellular ROS levels, while 
the peak in the combined PCT (2.5, 5, and 10 μM) treatment group was 
significantly shifted to the left (Fig. 6G and H), which also corresponded 
to a similar trend observed with Western blotting. 
3.9. BAY11-7082 reduces pyroptosis in HE-OC-1 cell induced by D-gal 
and LPS 
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) results showed (Fig. 8E) that 
the cell pyroptosis ostiole was enlarged in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal 
(30 mg/mL) treatment group, which was effectively alleviated in the 
group treated with PCT or BAY11–7082. The results of cell immuno­
fluorescence staining (Fig. 8A-D) showed that the intracellular Caspase- 
11 and GSDMD protein fluorescence intensity was significantly stronger 
in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treatment group than in the 
control group; this fluorescence intensity was significantly diminished in 
the combined PCT or BAY11–7082 treatment group. Western blotting 
results (Fig. 8F-K) showed that the expression of pyroptosis proteins 
Caspase-4/11, Caspase-1, and GSDMD as well as pro-inflammatory 
factors IL-1β and IL-18 were significantly increased in the LPS (1 μg/ 
mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treatment group compared with the control 
group, and their expression levels were inhibited in both PCT or 
BAY11–7082 treatment groups. 
3.10. BAY11-7082 reduces inflammatory injury and ROS levels in HE- 
OC-1 cells induced by D-gal and LPS 
To study the mechanism, we further verified the changes in intra­
cellular inflammation levels, and the results of cellular immunofluo­
rescence staining showed (Fig. 9A and B) that NLRP3 expression was 
increased in HEI-OC-1 cells in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) 
treatment group compared to the control group. Combined treatment 
with PCT or BAY11–7082 could inhibit the intracellular NLRP3 
expression. Western blotting analysis indicated (Fig. 9C-F) that the 
expression of NLRP3, NF-KB, and ASC showed similar trends with 
immunofluorescence staining levels. Compared with the control group, 
the expression was significantly increased in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal 
(30 mg/mL) treatment group and significantly decreased in the PCT or 
BAY11–7082 combined therapy group. Subsequently, ROS levels were 
measured using DCFH-DA fluorometric assay, and the results showed 
(Fig. 9I and J) that compared with the control group, ROS levels were 
significantly increased in HEI-OC-1 cells in the LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal 
(30 mg/mL) treatment group. Furthermore, they could be significantly 
reduced in the PCT (10 μM) and BAY11–7082 (5 μM) treatment groups, 
which was also confirmed by the flow cytometry results of ROS level 
measurement (Fig. 9G and H). These results suggest that PCT or 
BAY11–7082 can reduce intracellular inflammation and ROS levels.

deterioration is the most important challenge for humans to address as 
the population ages at an ever-increasing rate. The aging process of the 
auditory system is manifested by the loss of auditory input received from 
the periphery, and the deterioration of peripheral organs is caused in 
part by the loss of hair cells, SG neurons, and the dysfunction of the 
vascular striatum [24]. In contrast, loss of outer cochlear hair cells 
(OHCs) is thought to be the most persistent damage. ARHL is a common 
hearing loss model and C57BL/6 was labeled as a mouse model of ARHL 
as early as in the previous study. It was evident as early as 3 months of 
age. These mice gradually lose sensory hair cells (internal and external) 
in an age-dependent manner, from the base of the cochlea to the vertex 
[25]. In addition, they show loss of basal SG cells, loss of SL fibroblasts, 
and thinning of SV [25,26]. Our results showed that the expression of 
Caspase-11 and GSDMD-N were significantly up-regulated in 
12-month-old mice, and in addition, hair cells in inner ear structures, 
and SG neurons were damaged. PCT administration significantly 
reduced the structural damage of the inner ear and the accumulation of 
inflammation in vivo. Although the pathological features of ARHL are 
known to be distinct, its pathogenesis is still not fully understood and 
there are few effective treatments available. We added LPS + D-gal to 
simulate the inflammatory environment of inner ear hair cell senescence 
in our cellular experiments. Our results showed that the expression of 
Caspase-4/11 and GSDMD-N was upregulated in HEI-OC-1 cells after 
modeling, and SEM showed that cells in the LPS + D-gal group had 
significant pyroptosis ostiole, which was significantly attenuated by the 
administration of PCT. 
"Inflammatory aging" dominates the ARHL research field and pro­
vides a framework for therapeutic interventions [27]. On this basis 
various mechanisms and pathways of inflammation generation and 
deposition were established, such as ROS oxidative stress and activation 
of pyroptosis by the inflammatory vesicles [28–30]. In recent years, a 
growing body of evidence has focused on changes in inflammation and 
pyroptosis, which has led to new ideas for exploring the pathogenesis of 
ARHL [29]. PCT has been reported to have powerful anti-inflammatory 
and antioxidant effects [31–33]. In particular, PCT prevents 
LPS-induced cellular inflammation [34]. Our recent study shows that 
PCT can reduce scorching in vivo by inhibiting inflammatory vesicles in 
mice.In addition, recent studies have shown that PCT treatment reduces 
the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, thereby ameliorating 
D-GalN/LPS-induced liver injury in mice [35]. However, whether PCT 
protects against ARHL by modulating inflammatory pathways remains 
unknown. Therefore, we hypothesized that PCT may reduce inner ear 
hair cell damage and hearing loss by inhibiting inflammation and 
decreasing the activation of non-classical pyroptosis. In this study, the 
results showed that PCT significantly reduced hearing loss in aged mice, 
and in vitro experiments showed that PCT significantly alleviated the 
elevated levels of inflammation in HEI-OC-1 cells. The results also 
showed that PCT treatment significantly inhibited inflammation, 
reduced Caspase-11 expression, and reversed the non-classical pyrop­
tosis in aged mice. Our results suggest that PCT improves hearing loss 
and inner ear structural damage in aged mice by modulating the in­
flammatory response of inner ear hair cells and Caspase-4/11-mediated 
non-classical pyroptosis. 
There is a close relationship between oxidative stress and inflam­
matory aging, and ROS act as secondary messengers capable of regu­
lating pro-inflammatory gene expression [36], contributes to 
inflammatory aging and affects the body’s homeostasis and health. The 
relationship between redox status and immune cell function affects the 
rate of aging and lifespan [37,38]. In addition, antioxidants have been 
successfully applied to reduce oxidative stress damage and improve the

lifespan of individuals, which confirms the hypothesis of oxidative stress 
inflammation [39]. ROS play a major role in the inflammatory response, 
and ROS production is increased in excessive inflammatory responses. 
Under normal conditions, ROS are produced at low levels and play an 
important role in signal transduction, immune response and regulation 
of gene expression, but excessive accumulation of ROS may be toxic to 
cells [40]. We measured intracellular ROS levels in HEI-OC-1 cells after 
LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treatment, and found that intra­
cellular ROS levels were increased significantly after 48 h of both LPS 
and D-gal treatment, while PCT significantly reduced intracellular ROS 
levels. 
Caspase-11 expression was upregulated in the cochlea in the ARHL 
model. In addition to cleaving the GSDMD itself, Caspase-11 activates 
the pannexin 1 channel, which induces non-classical nucleotide-binding 
oligomerization domain-like receptor protein 3 (nucleotide-binding 
oligomerization domain-like receptor protein 3, NLRP3) assembly of 
inflammatory vesicles, and induces a series of inflammatory responses 
[41–43]. Many studies have shown that interventions that inhibit the 
accumulation of inflammation in vivo can be effective in alleviating 
ARHL [44]. We therefore used the inflammatory vesicle inhibitor 
BAY11–7082 to further validate the role of Caspase-11 in ARHL. Groups 
treated with PCT or the inflammatory vesicle inhibitor BAY11–7082 
showed a significant reduction in Caspase-11 and GSDMD-N expression 
in mice, attenuated ARHL, and structural damage and inflammatory 
accumulation in the cochlea was alleviated. Pretreatment of HEI-OC-1 
cells with PCT or the inflammatory vesicle inhibitor BAY11–7082 
inhibited NLRP3 inflammatory vesicle signaling activation in HEI-OC-1 
cells in in vitro experiments, causing a significant decrease in ASC and 
cell scorch execution protein GSDMD-N, IL-1β, and IL-18 protein 
expression. In conclusion, these results suggest that the role of PCT in 
attenuating 
ARHL-related 
injury 
involves 
Caspase-11-mediated 
non-classical pyroptosis. In addition, the present study suggests that 
upregulation of Caspase-11 expression may be one of the mechanisms 
supporting ARHL. The activated caspase-1 specifically cleaves the 
scorch execution protein GSDMD, and the cleaved N-terminal end of 
GSDMD can be inserted into the cell membrane to form a cell membrane 
pore, which leads to pyroptosis, and also promotes caspase-1-mediated 
maturation of IL-1β and IL-18, which aggravates the inflammatory 
response. Our findings suggest that inhibition of Caspase-11 expression 
alleviates ARHL and that the Caspase-11-mediated non-classical 
pyroptosis may be an effective target for the treatment of hearing loss in 
the elderly. 
LPS acts directly on Caspase-11 and directly activates the non- 
classical pyroptosis. Previous studies have investigated the protective 
effect of PCT on lipopolysaccharide (LPS) damage in mouse brain 
endothelial cell line (bEnd.3) cells and its potential mechanisms, 
demonstrating the inhibition of inflammation and oxidative stress by 
PCT [45]. Our results showed that pretreatment with PCT or the in­
flammatory vesicle inhibitor BAY11–7082 significantly inhibited the 
rise in ROS, as well as the inflammatory vesicle expression, reduced 
OC-1 cell injury, the accumulation of inflammation in OC-1 cells, and 
the activation of the downstream protein Caspase-11 that shears GSDMD 
causing a series of pyroptosis responses. In this study, we showed that 
PCT inhibited the release of Caspase-11 and GSDMD-N during ARHL via 
the Caspase-11 non-classical pyroptosis. In addition, PCT inhibited the 
expression of NLRP3, NF-KB, and ASC. These data suggest that PCT in­
hibits caspase-11 non-classical focal pathway signaling-mediated 
cellular pyroptosis through inhibition of inflammation and oxidative 
stress. 
Cysteinases are a family of conserved cysteine proteases that play a 
key role in programmed cell death and inflammation [46]. Inflamma­
tory caspase has been shown to play a central role in the innate immune 
response [47]. Caspase-11 as an inflammatory caspase can act directly as 
a receptor to sense pathogen-encoded molecules such as LPS, and sub­
sequently activate the inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, exacer­
bating the inflammatory response [48]. Previous studies have validated 
the relationship between aging, inflammation and pyroptosis in diabetic 
models [49]. In addition, the link between inflammation and pyroptosis 
has been demonstrated in various models such as myocardial infarction 
and pulmonary hypertension models [50]. Our studies in the ARHL 
model are also consistent with these findings that with aging, in vivo 
inflammation accumulates and cell pyroptosis occurs, and that inhibi­
tion of inflammatory cytokine release by PCT and elevation of ROS re­
duces cochlear structural damage and HEI-OC1 cell pyroptosis, and 
mitigates age-related hearing loss. 
5. Conclusions 
We established an ex vivo LPS + D-gal aging inflammation model 
and an in vitro geriatric model as a proxy for studying ARHL. We found 
that upregulation of Caspase-11 was associated with inflammation and 
promoted the onset of pyroptosis further exacerbating the inflammatory 
response. Interestingly, we demonstrated that PCT inhibited HEI-OC-1 
cells during ARHL with reduced inflammatory factor release, 
decreased ROS, and significantly reduced the levels of inflammation and 
pyroptosis-related protein expression. This study demonstrates that PCT 
treatment attenuates ARHL-induced inflammatory aging via the 
Caspase-11-mediated non-classical pyroptosis, thereby inhibiting the 
activation of pyroptosis and thus at least partially alleviating ARHL. 
Furthermore, our findings provide new insights into the protective 
mechanisms supporting the effects of PCT on ARHL and the basis of PCT 
in the clinical treatment of age-related diseases. However, our study has 
some limitations and further studies are needed to determine the ther­
apeutic potential of PCT via Caspase-11.

lifespan of individuals, which confirms the hypothesis of oxidative stress 
inflammation [39]. ROS play a major role in the inflammatory response, 
and ROS production is increased in excessive inflammatory responses. 
Under normal conditions, ROS are produced at low levels and play an 
important role in signal transduction, immune response and regulation 
of gene expression, but excessive accumulation of ROS may be toxic to 
cells [40]. We measured intracellular ROS levels in HEI-OC-1 cells after 
LPS (1 μg/mL) + D-gal (30 mg/mL) treatment, and found that intra­
cellular ROS levels were increased significantly after 48 h of both LPS 
and D-gal treatment, while PCT significantly reduced intracellular ROS 
levels. 
Caspase-11 expression was upregulated in the cochlea in the ARHL 
model. In addition to cleaving the GSDMD itself, Caspase-11 activates 
the pannexin 1 channel, which induces non-classical nucleotide-binding 
oligomerization domain-like receptor protein 3 (nucleotide-binding 
oligomerization domain-like receptor protein 3, NLRP3) assembly of 
inflammatory vesicles, and induces a series of inflammatory responses 
[41–43]. Many studies have shown that interventions that inhibit the 
accumulation of inflammation in vivo can be effective in alleviating 
ARHL [44]. We therefore used the inflammatory vesicle inhibitor 
BAY11–7082 to further validate the role of Caspase-11 in ARHL. Groups 
treated with PCT or the inflammatory vesicle inhibitor BAY11–7082 
showed a significant reduction in Caspase-11 and GSDMD-N expression 
in mice, attenuated ARHL, and structural damage and inflammatory 
accumulation in the cochlea was alleviated. Pretreatment of HEI-OC-1 
cells with PCT or the inflammatory vesicle inhibitor BAY11–7082 
inhibited NLRP3 inflammatory vesicle signaling activation in HEI-OC-1 
cells in in vitro experiments, causing a significant decrease in ASC and 
cell scorch execution protein GSDMD-N, IL-1β, and IL-18 protein 
expression. In conclusion, these results suggest that the role of PCT in 
attenuating 
ARHL-related 
injury 
involves 
Caspase-11-mediated 
non-classical pyroptosis. In addition, the present study suggests that 
upregulation of Caspase-11 expression may be one of the mechanisms 
supporting ARHL. The activated caspase-1 specifically cleaves the 
scorch execution protein GSDMD, and the cleaved N-terminal end of 
GSDMD can be inserted into the cell membrane to form a cell membrane 
pore, which leads to pyroptosis, and also promotes caspase-1-mediated 
maturation of IL-1β and IL-18, which aggravates the inflammatory 
response. Our findings suggest that inhibition of Caspase-11 expression 
alleviates ARHL and that the Caspase-11-mediated non-classical 
pyroptosis may be an effective target for the treatment of hearing loss in 
the elderly. 
LPS acts directly on Caspase-11 and directly activates the non- 
classical pyroptosis. Previous studies have investigated the protective 
effect of PCT on lipopolysaccharide (LPS) damage in mouse brain 
endothelial cell line (bEnd.3) cells and its potential mechanisms, 
demonstrating the inhibition of inflammation and oxidative stress by 
PCT [45]. Our results showed that pretreatment with PCT or the in­
flammatory vesicle inhibitor BAY11–7082 significantly inhibited the 
rise in ROS, as well as the inflammatory vesicle expression, reduced 
OC-1 cell injury, the accumulation of inflammation in OC-1 cells, and 
the activation of the downstream protein Caspase-11 that shears GSDMD 
causing a series of pyroptosis responses. In this study, we showed that 
PCT inhibited the release of Caspase-11 and GSDMD-N during ARHL via 
the Caspase-11 non-classical pyroptosis. In addition, PCT inhibited the 
expression of NLRP3, NF-KB, and ASC. These data suggest that PCT in­
hibits caspase-11 non-classical focal pathway signaling-mediated 
cellular pyroptosis through inhibition of inflammation and oxidative 
stress. 
Cysteinases are a family of conserved cysteine proteases that play a 
key role in programmed cell death and inflammation [46]. Inflamma­
tory caspase has been shown to play a central role in the innate immune 
response [47]. Caspase-11 as an inflammatory caspase can act directly as 
a receptor to sense pathogen-encoded molecules such as LPS, and sub­
sequently activate the inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, exacer­
bating the inflammatory response [48]. Previous studies have validated 
the relationship between aging, inflammation and pyroptosis in diabetic 
models [49]. In addition, the link between inflammation and pyroptosis 
has been demonstrated in various models such as myocardial infarction 
and pulmonary hypertension models [50]. Our studies in the ARHL 
model are also consistent with these findings that with aging, in vivo 
inflammation accumulates and cell pyroptosis occurs, and that inhibi­
tion of inflammatory cytokine release by PCT and elevation of ROS re­
duces cochlear structural damage and HEI-OC1 cell pyroptosis, and 
mitigates age-related hearing loss. 
5. Conclusions 
We established an ex vivo LPS + D-gal aging inflammation model 
and an in vitro geriatric model as a proxy for studying ARHL. We found 
that upregulation of Caspase-11 was associated with inflammation and 
promoted the onset of pyroptosis further exacerbating the inflammatory 
response. Interestingly, we demonstrated that PCT inhibited HEI-OC-1 
cells during ARHL with reduced inflammatory factor release, 
decreased ROS, and significantly reduced the levels of inflammation and 
pyroptosis-related protein expression. This study demonstrates that PCT 
treatment attenuates ARHL-induced inflammatory aging via the 
Caspase-11-mediated non-classical pyroptosis, thereby inhibiting the 
activation of pyroptosis and thus at least partially alleviating ARHL. 
Furthermore, our findings provide new insights into the protective 
mechanisms supporting the effects of PCT on ARHL and the basis of PCT 
in the clinical treatment of age-related diseases. However, our study has 
some limitations and further studies are needed to determine the ther­
apeutic potential of PCT via Caspase-11.

expression. In conclusion, these results suggest that the role of PCT in 
attenuating 
ARHL-related 
injury 
involves 
Caspase-11-mediated 
non-classical pyroptosis. In addition, the present study suggests that 
upregulation of Caspase-11 expression may be one of the mechanisms 
supporting ARHL. The activated caspase-1 specifically cleaves the 
scorch execution protein GSDMD, and the cleaved N-terminal end of 
GSDMD can be inserted into the cell membrane to form a cell membrane 
pore, which leads to pyroptosis, and also promotes caspase-1-mediated 
maturation of IL-1β and IL-18, which aggravates the inflammatory 
response. Our findings suggest that inhibition of Caspase-11 expression 
alleviates ARHL and that the Caspase-11-mediated non-classical 
pyroptosis may be an effective target for the treatment of hearing loss in 
the elderly. 
LPS acts directly on Caspase-11 and directly activates the non- 
classical pyroptosis. Previous studies have investigated the protective 
effect of PCT on lipopolysaccharide (LPS) damage in mouse brain 
endothelial cell line (bEnd.3) cells and its potential mechanisms, 
demonstrating the inhibition of inflammation and oxidative stress by 
PCT [45]. Our results showed that pretreatment with PCT or the in­
flammatory vesicle inhibitor BAY11–7082 significantly inhibited the 
rise in ROS, as well as the inflammatory vesicle expression, reduced 
OC-1 cell injury, the accumulation of inflammation in OC-1 cells, and 
the activation of the downstream protein Caspase-11 that shears GSDMD 
causing a series of pyroptosis responses. In this study, we showed that 
PCT inhibited the release of Caspase-11 and GSDMD-N during ARHL via 
the Caspase-11 non-classical pyroptosis. In addition, PCT inhibited the 
expression of NLRP3, NF-KB, and ASC. These data suggest that PCT in­
hibits caspase-11 non-classical focal pathway signaling-mediated 
cellular pyroptosis through inhibition of inflammation and oxidative 
stress. 
Cysteinases are a family of conserved cysteine proteases that play a 
key role in programmed cell death and inflammation [46]. Inflamma­
tory caspase has been shown to play a central role in the innate immune 
response [47]. Caspase-11 as an inflammatory caspase can act directly as 
a receptor to sense pathogen-encoded molecules such as LPS, and sub­
sequently activate the inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, exacer­
bating the inflammatory response [48]. Previous studies have validated 
the relationship between aging, inflammation and pyroptosis in diabetic 
models [49]. In addition, the link between inflammation and pyroptosis 
has been demonstrated in various models such as myocardial infarction 
and pulmonary hypertension models [50]. Our studies in the ARHL 
model are also consistent with these findings that with aging, in vivo 
inflammation accumulates and cell pyroptosis occurs, and that inhibi­
tion of inflammatory cytokine release by PCT and elevation of ROS re­
duces cochlear structural damage and HEI-OC1 cell pyroptosis, and 
mitigates age-related hearing loss. 
5. Conclusions 
We established an ex vivo LPS + D-gal aging inflammation model 
and an in vitro geriatric model as a proxy for studying ARHL. We found 
that upregulation of Caspase-11 was associated with inflammation and 
promoted the onset of pyroptosis further exacerbating the inflammatory 
response. Interestingly, we demonstrated that PCT inhibited HEI-OC-1 
cells during ARHL with reduced inflammatory factor release, 
decreased ROS, and significantly reduced the levels of inflammation and 
pyroptosis-related protein expression. This study demonstrates that PCT 
treatment attenuates ARHL-induced inflammatory aging via the 
Caspase-11-mediated non-classical pyroptosis, thereby inhibiting the 
activation of pyroptosis and thus at least partially alleviating ARHL. 
Furthermore, our findings provide new insights into the protective 
mechanisms supporting the effects of PCT on ARHL and the basis of PCT 
in the clinical treatment of age-related diseases. However, our study has 
some limitations and further studies are needed to determine the ther­
apeutic potential of PCT via Caspase-11.
